source.name: Institute on Taxation & Economic Policy (ITEP), Who Pays? 7th Edition
source.data_year_note: Reflects 2024 tax law in Ohio, presented at 2023 income levels.

scope: Effective Ohio STATE and LOCAL taxes as a share of family income. Includes state income tax, state and local sales taxes, and property taxes (direct and passed-through to renters). Does NOT include federal taxes.
modeling_note: This is an ITEP incidence model for Ohio statewide. Cincinnati's mix of taxes (e.g. a higher flat local income tax than many Ohio cities) differs from the Ohio average. When applied to Cincinnati-specific income percentile thresholds, this should be treated as a reasonable estimate of the state+local tax burden pattern, not a measurement for any specific Cincinnati household.
key_finding: Ohio's state and local tax system is regressive: the lowest-income 20% of households pay more than twice the share of their income in state and local taxes (12.7%) that the top 1% pays (6.3%).
caveats: ITEP data reflects Ohio statewide averages. Cincinnati residents face a different mix than an Ohio county without a municipal income tax.
Federal taxes are NOT included. Adding federal incidence (which is progressive) produces a different overall picture. See CBO historical effective federal rates for national figures.
Property taxes are partially modeled as passed through to renters. The magnitude of pass-through is debated in the public finance literature.
The 'Lowest 20%' figure does not include government transfers (SNAP, EITC refunds, housing subsidies). A post-transfer, post-tax lens looks different and is not represented here.
